pantalla_bienvenida.parrafo1: There are painters who paint pictures and painters who paint cities. Pineda was one of the latter.
pantalla_bienvenida.parrafo2: For 66 years he left on canvases and papers what most of us see without looking at: the whitewash of a wall that only exists at four in the afternoon, the green of the Guadalquivir in January, the empty square that has more life than when it is full.
pantalla_bienvenida.texto_pineda:
Some painters paint pictures. Others paint cities. Pineda was the second kind.

For 66 years he captured on canvas and paper what most of us look at without seeing: the whitewash of a wall that only exists at four in the afternoon, the green of the Guadalquivir in January, the empty square that holds more life than when it's full.

Born in Capuchinos in 1931, he died in February 2009 without making much noise. He had simply painted. And Córdoba, always suicidal, buried another of its geniuses alive. What remained slept in a dark storeroom where his family keeps a body of work that deserves an avenue. At best they'll name a back alley after him.

Thirteen stops. Thirteen reasons to walk, enter, and stand in front of a painting thinking: I've seen this before, but never like this.

anzuelos.inicio_p1: A palace with twelve courtyards and five hundred years of secrets. Pineda walked in and saw the whole of Córdoba in a single whitewashed wall.
anzuelos.p1_p2: A palace with twelve courtyards and five hundred years of secrets. Pineda entered here and saw all of Córdoba in a single whitewashed wall.
anzuelos.p2_p3: The Santa Marina neighborhood smells of bullfighting. Manolete was born two streets away. Pineda painted it without painting it: the shadow of a cape is enough.
anzuelos.p3_p4: Since 1850, guitarists and poets have been drinking at La Fuenseca without anyone inviting them. Performances here are not programmed: they happen by chance.
anzuelos.p4_p5: Here is the second room. The one that changes everything you thought you knew about how Pineda paints.
anzuelos.p5_p6: Since 1880, El Pisto has seen Manolete pass, Julio Romero de Torres and Córdoba's rogues. They all drank at the same bar.
anzuelos.p6_p7: There are taverns that serve wine. And taverns that guard paintings that the owner would never sell at any price.
anzuelos.p7_p8: The Judería cannot be explained: you have to walk it. Every cobblestone has three civilizations beneath it. Pineda painted them all at once.
anzuelos.p8_p9: A fifteenth-century house next to the Royal Stables. Here it smells of Córdoba courtyards and timeless cooking.
anzuelos.p9_p10: San Basilio is the neighborhood of courtyards. The gate that closes it to the south has been letting pass those who know how to look for centuries.
anzuelos.p10_p11: They say La Viuda keeps the coldest salmorejo in Córdoba and the hardest painting to see on the route.
anzuelos.p11_p12: Nearly there. Two doors from La Inaudita there's a cazuela named after the trade that filled this street for centuries.
anzuelos.p12_p13: The final gallery. Where it all began. Here is the exhibition's heart, and its most important work.

pantalla_intermedia.instruccion: When you arrive, open the camera and point it at the Pineda sign at the entrance

salas.viana_gancho: Pineda didn't paint monuments. He painted what monuments remember.
salas.casa12pb_gancho: The painter Córdoba knew, and the painter Córdoba ignored. Here is the second one.
salas.inaudita_gancho: Bulls, women, death. In Córdoba, they are the same thing in different clothes.
salas.viana_texto: Pineda painted Córdoba the way you take stock of what you love before you die. Not the tourist's inventory — the one who collects postcards and pretty angles — but the person who has walked every street so many times they no longer look at it, they remember it.
salas.casa12pb_texto: What's in this gallery was never sold. It never hung in any tavern. It waited in silence for decades for someone curious enough to open it.
salas.inaudita_texto: There are many ways to face a bull and very few ways to get close. Pineda understood danger. The kind that doesn't warn you.
